primary_diagnosis.name: Catheter-associated obstructive urinary tract infection with impending urosepsis (neurogenic bladder secondary to spina bifida)

comorbidities.name: Spina bifida (repaired lumbosacral myelomeningocele) with flaccid paraplegia
Neurogenic bladder managed with long-term indwelling urethral catheter
Neurogenic bowel with chronic constipation (4-day faecal loading)

pathophysiology_summary: A long-term indwelling Foley catheter in a neurogenic bladder became occluded (encrustation plus external compression from 4 days of faecal loading), so infected urine could no longer drain. Urine backed up into a distended, colonised bladder, and the trapped infected urine under pressure seeded the bloodstream, producing a systemic inflammatory response (high fever, rigors, tachycardia, borderline hypotension) — impending urosepsis and septic shock. The definitive treatment is mechanical: relieve the obstruction by changing the blocked catheter (draining the infected residual) AND commence parenteral empirical antibiotics, plus aggressively treat the constipation that precipitated the block. An obstructed, infected urinary tract is a urological emergency because it deteriorates to septic shock within hours if the obstruction is not relieved. Breath-Ezy (telehealth) can do NEITHER the catheter change NOR parenteral antibiotics, so its only safe role is to recognise sepsis behind an obstructed infected system and escalate immediately, communicating the severe latex allergy so responders bring latex-free equipment.

differential.diagnosis: Obstructive catheter-associated UTI with impending urosepsis
differential.evidence_basis: Blocked catheter with no drainage + high fever + rigors + severe suprapubic pain is a classic obstructed-infected-urinary-tract picture.
Recurrent UTIs are common in this patient, but rigors, systemic toxicity and a non-draining catheter place this beyond a simple UTI.

differential.diagnosis: Acute urinary retention / bladder distension from faecal loading alone
differential.evidence_basis: Severe constipation can block a catheter and cause pain, but does not by itself explain fever and rigors.
Any febrile illness in a shunted patient must screen for shunt sepsis — but no headache, vomiting or altered consciousness here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the obstructed-infected system should already lead; a simple-UTI anchor that ignores the rigors and non-draining catheter is the under-triage trap.

differential.diagnosis: Obstructive catheter-associated UTI with impending urosepsis / septic shock
differential.evidence_basis: Fever + rigors + tachycardia + borderline hypotension + lightheadedness on sitting behind a non-draining infected catheter = sepsis with early circulatory compromise.
No headache, vomiting, drowsiness or altered mental state; shunt tract non-tender — infection is clearly localised to the obstructed bladder.
Pain is central over the distended bladder and correlates exactly with the mechanical catheter failure, not the right iliac fossa.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Circulatory-compromise screen (DI-007) confirms this is sepsis with early shock — mandates emergency escalation, not home antibiotics.

differential.diagnosis: Catheter-associated obstructive urinary tract infection with impending urosepsis
differential.evidence_basis: Obstructed, infected neurogenic bladder with systemic sepsis; requires urgent obstruction relief (catheter change) plus parenteral antibiotics — neither deliverable via telehealth.

red_flags.description: High fever with rigors, tachycardia and systemic toxicity — systemic inflammatory / septic response
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rigors with a source that cannot drain signals bacteraemia/sepsis; time-critical.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to septic shock, multi-organ failure and death within hours.

red_flags.description: Obstructed indwelling catheter — no urine draining, infected urine bypassing under pressure
red_flags.clinical_significance: An obstructed, infected urinary tract is a urological emergency; the obstruction must be physically relieved.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Rising back-pressure seeds the bloodstream and accelerates septic shock; retention can also injure the upper tracts.

red_flags.description: Borderline hypotension (BP 100/60) with tachycardia (HR 125) and lightheadedness on sitting — early distributive (septic) shock
red_flags.clinical_significance: The measured numbers are clinician-obtained, but the patient-reportable lightheadedness is the telehealth proxy for early shock.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognition of shock delays fluids and source control.

red_flags.description: Severe latex allergy — anaphylaxis risk if a latex catheter or latex equipment is used during the emergency catheter change
red_flags.clinical_significance: Must be communicated to responders so only 100% silicone / latex-free equipment is used.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Iatrogenic latex anaphylaxis on top of urosepsis.

red_flags.description: VP shunt in a febrile patient — shunt infection/malfunction must be actively screened, not assumed absent
red_flags.clinical_significance: Negative here (no headache/vomiting/altered mental state, non-tender tract) but is a mandatory screen and a re-escalation trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed shunt infection/blockage is rapidly life-threatening.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the new fever, pain and distress to the patient's baseline disability or 'just another UTI' rather than working it up as an acute treatable emergency (obstructed infected system with sepsis).
Treating this as an uncomplicated recurrent UTI and prescribing oral antibiotics for home, missing the rigors, systemic toxicity and the non-draining obstructed catheter that make it sepsis.

Attempting to manage at home because the patient is hard to transport — the definitive treatment (catheter change + parenteral antibiotics) cannot be delivered remotely, so the only safe action is escalation.
Escalating without flagging the severe latex allergy, risking a latex catheter being used and precipitating anaphylaxis.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: elevated concern — tachycardia and borderline hypotension with an obvious infective source; formal scoring needs clinician-measured BP/RR/GCS
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports sepsis with early haemodynamic compromise; mandates emergency assessment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact blood pressure, heart rate and respiratory rate (clinician-measured: BP 100/60, HR 125, RR 24, SpO2 97%)
Palpation of the distended, tender bladder extending to the umbilicus
Palpation of firm faecal loading in the left lower quadrant and bowel sounds
Genitourinary examination confirming the occluded 16Fr Foley with purulent bypassing urine
Neurological confirmation of baseline sensory level (below L2) and flaccid paraplegia
Examination of the VP shunt tract for tenderness/erythema
Urine microscopy/culture, blood cultures, lactate, white cell count and CRP
The volume of residual infected urine drained on catheter change (900 mL on the in-person visit)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise sepsis behind an obstructed infected urinary tract and escalate immediately — call 000 for an ambulance (patient is wheelchair-dependent and cannot easily self-transport), explicitly tell responders about the severe latex allergy so latex-free catheter equipment is used, and do NOT attempt home antibiotic management. Definitive care = urgent catheter change to relieve the obstruction + parenteral empirical antibiotics + IV fluids in a facility.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the presence of a serious infection; the real risk is UNDER-action — mislabelling as a simple UTI, managing at home with oral antibiotics, or attributing the deterioration to the baseline disability (diagnostic overshadowing).

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australian Sepsis Network — Recognising and responding to sepsis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Fever with rigors and haemodynamic change plus an obstructed infective source warrants emergency escalation and source control, not community oral antibiotics.
Catheter-associated UTI with systemic features and obstruction requires catheter change (source control) plus parenteral empirical therapy.
